old_claim.note: Maritime Law Codes provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Transport-amphora standards scaled through Mediterranean trade: type catalogues record origin, distribution, contents, and capacity because the containers moved commodities across trade networks.

source_shape.source_locator: Catalogue introduction and Agora F65-66 details: the resource organizes Roman amphora types by distinctive features, date range, origin, distribution, contents, and capacity; the example type is distributed across Athens, Ephesus, the eastern Mediterranean, Ostia, and western provinces.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source treats amphora types as trade containers characterized by production origin, distribution, contents, capacity, and date range.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A shipping-container standard is commercially meaningful because containers circulate through trade networks; maritime law may regulate disputes but does not create the ceramic form or capacity standard.

ontology_before: Amphora Shipping Standards depended on Maritime Law Codes, implying legal codes enabled the container standard.
ontology_after: Amphora Shipping Standards depend on pottery production plus trade-route scaling, while maritime law remains a separate legal technology.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: amphora_shipping_standards now depends on trade_routes and no longer directly depends on maritime_law_codes.
would_reject_if: The node were broadened back to every ancient amphora or storage jar rather than source-backed transport-amphora standards.
A source showed legal maritime codes directly specified the earliest amphora forms, capacities, or stamps.
The node were split into Greek stamped handles, Roman Dressel types, and storage amphorae with separate prerequisites.
short_reason: Trade-route circulation scales transport amphora standards; maritime law does not enable the ceramic standard.
